coredata.dc:title: Thermal and rheological behavior of aluminium-doped zinc oxide nanofluids: Experimental study and application of artificial neural network model
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jeyaraj, A.
Sakthivel, P.
Saravanakumar, K.
Arivalagan, Aravinth Raj
Karthik, V.
Thirumurugan, Arun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the influence of Al-doping concentration on the thermal and rheological characteristics of pristine zinc oxide (ZnO) nanofluids was investigated across a range of temperatures and nanoparticle concentrations. A multilayer perceptron (MLP) artificial neural network (ANN) with an optimized topology was employed to model the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids. The addition of 0.13 M of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles at 1 % rate led to a remarkable 64 % increase in the thermal conductivity of the base fluid (50:50 ratio water + ethylene glycol mixture). Furthermore, incorporating 1 % of these Al-doped nanoparticles outperformed pure zinc oxide, resulting in a 44 % boost in thermal conductivity. The enhanced heat transfer capabilities of Al-doped zinc oxide were evident across different doping and nanoparticle concentrations. The viscosity of nanofluids increases with an increase in nanoparticle concentration and decreases with an increase in temperature. ZnO and Al-doped ZnO nanoparticles have been prepared and studied their structural, morphological and elemental properties using XRD, SEM and EDS respectively. The predicted thermal and rheological behaviours are in close agreement with the experimental values.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluids
Thermal conductivity
Viscosity
ZnO
Al
ANN

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal conductivity: effect of nanoparticle concentration and temperature
Viscosity: effect of nanoparticle concentration and temperature
ANN modeling of thermal conductivity and viscosity
Optimization of the best multilayer perceptron (MLP) model topology
R-squared and MSE results for 40 experimental data sets of thermal conductivity and viscosity using MLP with optimized hidden neurons (6 hidden neurons)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Variation of thermal conductivity with respect to weight percentage at room and high temperature.
Variation of viscosity with respect to weight percentage at room and high temperature.
XRD patterns of pure zinc oxide and aluminium doped zinc oxide nanoparticles.
SEM images of (a) Pure zinc oxide, (b) ZnO:Al(0.018 M), (c) ZnO:Al(0.05 M), (d) ZnO:Al(0.07 M) and(e) ZnO:Al(0.13 M).
EDAX spectra of Al (0.13 M) doped ZnO nanoparticles.
A flow chart of the simulation section.
Variation of overall R2 and MSE with respect to number of hidden neurons for (a) Thermal conductivityand (b) Viscosity.
Training, Validation and Testing results between experimental and predicted values for (a) Thermal conductivity and (b) Viscosity.
Errors of ANN (with optimized hidden neuron).
Comparison of results with published articles.

row.entry: S.No
Investigation
Findings
Reference
Rheological behavior of water–carbon nanotube/magnetite nanofluid subjected to a magnetic field
Presence of an external magnetic field causes an increase in the viscosity of the water. viscosity augments by boosting the concentration of the nano-materials
Thermal conductivity of SiO2/water-ethylene glycol (50:50) hybrid nanofluid using artificial neural network
ANN showed better performance and better correlation and also smaller error in most of the predicted data points
ANNs for prediction of the thermal characteristics of water/ethylene glycol-based nanofluids containing MWCNTs, titania, and zinc oxide
Overall absolute error value was less than 0.018 for the 102 datasets. A fitting method is also applied and the surface is generated that illustrates the conducting behavior of nanofluid.
Thermal conductivity and viscosity of Al2O3–ZnO-MWCNT-EG ternary nanofluid
Thermal conductivity was determined for a temperature range of 25–40 °C and viscosity was measured for a temperature range of 40–70 °C
Stability, optical property and thermal conductivity of water based MWCNT- Al2O3–ZnO mono, binary and ternary nanofluid
Thermal conductivity increased with the increase in volume concentration of nanoparticles for mono and binary nanofluid sample
Dynamic viscosity of MWCNTWO3/water-ethylene glycol antifreeze hybrid nanofluid
As the volume fraction increases at a constant temperature, the dynamic velocity increases
Rheological behavior predictions of non-Newtonian nanofluids via correlations and ANN for thermal applications
Nano-suspension properties are highly dependent on nanoparticle concentrations, temperatures, and shear rates. All three inputs are influential to the nanofluids' viscosity
Dynamic Viscosity of nanofluids using artificial neural network and genetic algorithm.
Dynamic viscosity of water-based nanofluids containing Al2O3, TiO2, and ZnO nanoparticles was experimentally measured and predicted with multi-layer perceptron ANN
Thermal conductivity of hybrid nanofluids: Application of a committee of robust neural networks, theoretical, and empirical models
Predicting TC coefficient of hybrid nanofluids, boasting an average absolute percent relative error of just 1.064 %.
Dynamic viscosity of Hybrid nano-lubricant containing Zinc Oxide in Commercial oil
ANN are similar to the targets close to μnf of MWCNT-ZnO (50–50)/oil SAE50 nano-lubricant

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: List of Chemicals used and their physical/chemical properties.

row.entry: Material Used
Physical Properties
Chemical Properties

Zinc Acetate Di-hydrate
Purity: 99 %
Make: CHEM-LAB

Appearance: White crystalline solid.
Molecular Weight: 219.50 g/mol.
Melting Point: 237 °C.
Solubility: Soluble in water.

Zinc acetate di-hydrate is a zinc salt of acetic acid.
It is often used as a catalyst in organic synthesis.

Aluminium Nitrate
Purity: 98.5 %
Make: CHEM-LAB

Appearance: White crystalline solid.
Molecular Weight: 213.01 g/mol.
Melting Point: 150–180 °C.
Solubility: Soluble in water

Aluminum nitrate is a salt of aluminum and nitric acid.
It is used in the production of aluminum hydroxide and other chemical processes.

Sodium Hydroxide
Purity: 99 %
Make: CHEM-LAB

Appearance: White solid, typically pellets
Molecular Weight: 40.00 g/mol. Melting Point: 318 °C.
Solubility: Highly soluble in water

Sodium hydroxide is a strong base widely used in the chemical industry.
Used as a cleaning agent, pH regulator, and in the production of various chemicals.

Polyvinyl-Pyrrolidone
Purity:99 %
Make: CHEM-LAB

Appearance: White to off-white powder/liquid.
Molecular Weight: Varies. Solubility: Soluble in water and solvents.

PVP is a polymer with various molecular weights.
It is often used as binding/stabilizing agent.

Ethylene Glycol
Purity: 99 %
Make: CHEM-LAB

Appearance: Colorless, odorless liquid.
Molecular Weight: 62.07 g/mol. Melting Point: −13.2 °C.
Boiling Point: 197.3 °C
Ethylene glycol is used as a coolant, antifreeze, and in the production of plastics and resins

head.ce:title: Thermal and rheological behavior of aluminium-doped zinc oxide nanofluids: Experimental study and application of artificial neural network model

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the influence of Al-doping concentration on the thermal and rheological characteristics of pristine zinc oxide (ZnO) nanofluids was investigated across a range of temperatures and nanoparticle concentrations. A multilayer perceptron (MLP) artificial neural network (ANN) with an optimized topology was employed to model the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids. The addition of 0.13 M of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles at 1 % rate led to a remarkable 64 % increase in the thermal conductivity of the base fluid (50:50 ratio water + ethylene glycol mixture). Furthermore, incorporating 1 % of these Al-doped nanoparticles outperformed pure zinc oxide, resulting in a 44 % boost in thermal conductivity. The enhanced heat transfer capabilities of Al-doped zinc oxide were evident across different doping and nanoparticle concentrations. The viscosity of nanofluids increases with an increase in nanoparticle concentration and decreases with an increase in temperature. ZnO and Al-doped ZnO nanoparticles have been prepared and studied their structural, morphological and elemental properties using XRD, SEM and EDS respectively. The predicted thermal and rheological behaviours are in close agreement with the experimental values.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscience research has emerged as a pivotal frontier in the development of cutting-edge technologies. Despite its cost-effectiveness and widespread availability as a heat transfer medium, water's heat transfer efficiency falls short of meeting the demands of modern applications. The relatively low thermal conductivity of water, at 0.6 W/mK, and its unsuitable melting and boiling points for automotive and electrical heat transfer applications primarily cause this limitation. Adding ethylene glycol to water can alter its phase change behavior, but for each proportion of ethylene glycol added, this modification reduces the thermal conductivity of water by half. In response to this issue, researchers developed and commercially available nanofluids to enhance heat transmission. Choi et al. [1] coined the term "nanofluids" to describe fluids that disperse nanoparticles within a base fluid, which can include ethylene glycols, water, polymers, bio-fluids, oil solutions, or various mixtures thereof. Nanofluids play a vital role in numerous applications, including heat transfer [2], nuclear systems [3], electrical and electronic devices [4], fuel cells [5], and solar panels [6], owing to their unique properties. There are many ways to make nanoparticles, such as chemical vapour deposition [7], chemical vapour condensation [8], simple soft chemical route [9], flame spray pyrolysis [10], sonochemical-precipitation method [11], direct oxidation, co-precipitation, and sol-gel processes [12]. Among these, the co-precipitation method is noteworthy for its cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness.

Ceramic nanoparticles generally exhibit superior heat transfer capabilities compared to other nanoparticle classes. We usually mix oxides [13], nitrides [14], carbides [15], graphene [16], carbon nanotubes [17], and other substances with a base fluid to create ceramic-based nanofluids. Ceramic-based nanofluids frequently choose zinc oxide due to its stability and antibacterial properties [18]. Haghshenasfard et al. [19] found that using zinc oxide-dispersed nanofluids instead of pure water as the base fluid made the heat transfer coefficient 20 % better. In applications involving heat transfer, both thermal and viscous properties are critical. The rheological behaviour of pure ZnO and Al-doped ZnO nanofluids must also be considered. Numerous researchers have explored the rheological properties of nanofluids. As an example, Lee et al. [20] looked into the viscosity of ZnO/EG nanofluids and saw Newtonian behaviour between 20 and 80 °C and for nanoparticle volumes ranging from 0.5 % to 5.5 %. The temperature dependence of viscosity varies across studies [21,22], with some reporting a decrease in viscosity with increasing temperature, while others indicate an increase. Overall, when temperatures are high, changes in the speed of fluid molecules can affect the pushing or pulling forces between molecules, which can cause changes in viscosity that depend on temperature.

It is worth mentioning that undoped ZnO is a special kind of energy band gap semiconductor that has native flaws like oxygen vacancies and zinc interstitials [23,24]. However, its conductivity behavior is thermally unstable due to a lack of free electrons. Group III ions such as Al3+, B3+, Ga3+, and In3+ replace Zn2+ ions to overcome this issue [25,26]. This increases the number of free electrons in charge carriers and improves the material's electrical, thermal, optical, and magnetic properties. Group III ions frequently employ Al3+ due to its low material cost and small ionic radius. Generally, doping of Al3+ ions into zinc oxide is shown to increase electron concentration [27,28]. Aravinth Raj et al. [29] achieved an amazing 37.28 % increase in thermal conductivity by using silver and aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanofluids. The nanofluids had a concentration of 1 % nanoparticles and were made up of 50:50 water and ethylene glycol. An experimental investigation has been made to discuss the natural convection of ZnO/Water Nanofluids in a square cavity and the heat transfer coefficient has been enhanced by 9.14 % [30].

Accurate prediction of thermal and viscous behaviour in nanofluids is of paramount importance and relies on mathematical models and advanced ANN models. Hamilton and Crosser's model is a classical tool for predicting nanofluid thermal behaviour [31]. Other conventional models, such as Wasp, Yu, and Choi [32], and Murshed [33] are available for calculating nanofluid thermal conductivities. The models of Brinkman [34] and Wang [35] are used for empirically predicting the viscous behaviour of nanofluids. However, these models have inherent limitations, including discrepancies in the R-squared value between experimental and predicted values. To overcome these limitations, there is a growing need for robust Artificial Neural Network (ANN) technology, which can extrapolate long-term data from limited experimental data [36]. Recent works published in the area of the proposed work have been compared and listed in a Table .1 .

Despite significant advancements in the field of nanofluids, a substantial research gap remains in the comprehensive exploration of higher concentrations of aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles. Our study aims to address this gap. The majority of previous studies have primarily focused on lower doping concentrations, limiting the scope of their practical application. This has been addressed in the current study. A strong Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model that has been employed with an optimized topology to make accurate predictions of thermal and viscous behaviour of nanofluids.

In the present study, we address a key gap in the existing body of research by systematically investigating higher concentrations of aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles in nanofluids, a less explored area with significant potential for advanced heat transfer applications. The novelty of our work lies in demonstrating a substantial enhancement in thermal conductivity, achieving up to a 64 % increase compared to the base fluid, and applying an optimized ANN model to predict both thermal conductivity and viscosity. The objective of this research is to evaluate the thermal and rheological properties of these novel nanofluids under varying doping concentrations and temperatures, and to validate the effectiveness of ANN models in capturing these complex behaviors with high accuracy. This work not only improves upon prior studies that focused on lower doping concentrations but also provides a robust methodology for predicting nanofluid behavior in practical applications.

The reagents zinc acetate dihydrate (99 % purity), aluminium nitrate (98.5 % purity), sodium hydroxide, polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP), and ethylene glycol were purchased from CHEM-LAB. All chemicals are without further purification. Table 2 lists the physical and chemical properties of all materials.

A solution of zinc acetate dihydrate with a molarity of 0.6 M, sodium hydroxide with a molarity of 1 M, and various concentrations of aluminium nitrate solution (0.018 M, 0.05 M, 0.07 M, and 0.13 M) were meticulously prepared and taken into account. The aluminium nitrate solutions were deliberately varied in concentration to establish a robust Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model. 0.6 M of zinc acetate di-hydrate and 0.018 M of aluminium nitrate solution were taken in a beaker in a ratio of 5:1. Titrating the solution by adding 1 M sodium hydroxide drop by drop under vigorous stirring. The titration procedure is repeated until the solution turns milky white. During this process, zinc acetate and sodium hydroxide reacted, creating zinc hydroxide precipitates. Aluminium nitrate (Al3+ ions) filled some of the zinc interstitial spaces in the zinc hydroxide structure. The resulting solution was washed four times with distilled water to eliminate the residues of sodium acetate and sodium nitrate. The Al-doped zinc hydroxide settled, while sodium acetate and sodium nitrate, along with distilled water, formed froth on the surface. After removing the froth, we heated the Al-doped zinc hydroxide solution at 150 °C for 10 h to remove water molecules, forming agglomerated precipitates of Al-doped zinc oxide. After 60 min of mechanical milling, the nanoparticles of Al-doped zinc oxide was obtained. This entire process was replicated using different molarities of aluminium nitrate (0.05 M, 0.07 M, and 0.13 M). A pure zinc oxide sample was prepared using the same method, omitting the addition of aluminium nitrate, to evaluate the thermal conductivity enhancement of aluminiumdoped zinc oxide compared to pure zinc oxide.

A mixture of base fluids, consisting of a 50:50 ratio of water and ethylene glycol, served as the starting point. Various weight percentages of Al-doped and pure zinc oxide nanoparticles (0.25 %, 0.5 %, 0.75 %, and 1.0 %) were uniformly dispersed into this base fluid. For nanoparticle dispersion, we used a high-intensity probe sonicator operating at a frequency of 20 kHz and an amplitude of 50 %. The sonication process lasted for 15 min at a controlled temperature of 25 °C to prevent excessive heating. These parameters were determined through preliminary experiments to achieve optimal nanoparticle dispersion. To prevent undesirable nanoparticle agglomeration, polyvinyl-pyrrolidone (PVP) was introduced into the base fluid at concentrations ranging from 1 % to 5 %. After a series of experiments, a 3 % PVP concentration was found to result in the least sedimentation and agglomeration after 72 h of stability testing. PVP attached to the particle surfaces, creating strong repulsive forces between closely situated particles, thereby enhancing the overall stability of the nanofluids.

In this study, the maximum observed stability time for the nanofluid extended to an impressive 96 h (4 days), with no visible signs of sedimentation or phase separation. This remarkable stability was achieved using optimized parameters: a 3 % PVP concentration and 15 min of sonication at 25 °C. A total of twenty distinct experimental nanofluids were meticulously prepared for the subsequent examination of their thermal and viscous behaviours.

The crystal structure and particle dimensions of both pure zinc oxide and various concentrations of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles were systematically characterized using a powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD-PANalytical X'Pert). This analysis utilized copper K-α radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at 50 kV and 200 mA. The shape and size of the nanoparticles were examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) with a 10 kV operating voltage (model: JEOL-JSM 6390).Investigations to assess the thermal properties of the nanofluids were conducted at both room temperature and elevated temperatures, varying the nanoparticle weight percentage. These experiments were performed using a specially designed setup featuring a temperature-regulated chamber. Thermal conductivity measurements were carried out using the KD2-Pro thermal conductivity analyzer-probe, suitable for a range of temperatures, ensuring accurate readings under varying thermal conditions. Prior to each experimental session, the setup was calibrated using a water reference standard with a known thermal conductivity of 0.6 W/mK at room temperature. The viscous properties of the nanofluids were examined by measuring their viscosity with a vibrational viscometer. Calibration of the viscometer, similar to the KD2-Pro, was performed against standard fluids with known viscosity values before each experimental session, ensuring accuracy within the instrument's specified range. The average of three measurements per condition was reported for both thermal conductivity and viscosity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity: effect of nanoparticle concentration and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: A commercial combination mixture of 50:50 ratios of distilled water and ethylene glycol has a thermal conductivity value of 0.322 W/mK at room temperature. The thermal conductivity of pure zinc oxide dispersed nanofluid is higher than the base fluid of water and ethylene glycol mixture. This may be attributed to the Brownian motion of nanoparticles and the formation of liquid layers around the nanoparticles [37], which are responsible for enhancing thermal conductivity. The thermal conductivity of Al-doped zinc oxide dispersed nanofluid is greater than that of pure zinc oxide, and its value increases with increasing doping concentration. This may be the result of Al3+ ions occupying Zn2+ ion sites and generating more charge carriers responsible for the increase in conductivity [27,28]. Fig. 1 shows, under all the conditions of pure zinc oxide and different concentrations of Al-doped zinc oxide nanofluids, thermal conductivity at ambient temperature and high temperature increases with an increase in the weight % of nanoparticles addition. Adding 0.13 M of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles to the base fluid at ambient temperature resulted in a 64 % increase in thermal conductivity over the base fluid mixture of water and ethylene glycol. Thus, increasing the quantity of nanoparticles in the base fluid speeds up Brownian motion by increasing the rate of collisions among the nanoparticles. The variation of viscosity and thermal conductivity of pure and Al-doped ZnO nanofluids in room temperature and high temperature are presented in Table 3 .

Many practical applications rely on the knowledge of thermal conductivity variation with temperature. All of the nanoparticle dispersion settings, increasing temperature enhanced thermal conductivities. Adding 1 percent of 0.13 M aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles to the base fluid at a high temperature (52 °C) increased thermal conductivity by 13 percent compared to room temperature (Fig. 1). Based on the Brownian motion, nanoparticles move in lockstep with the molecules of the fluid they are suspended in. During the motion, particles collide with each other and facilitate solid-to-solid heat transfer. Therefore, Brownian motion itself is a diffusive process with a diffusive constant "D". This diffusive constant is a strong temperature function. As the temperature rises, the diffusion constant increases, leading to an increase in thermal conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viscosity: effect of nanoparticle concentration and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The density of nanofluids increases with both doping and nanoparticle concentration. Consequently, the viscosity of nanofluids experiences a noteworthy increase as nanoparticle concentration rises at ambient temperature and elevated temperature, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . However, it is essential to note that viscosity decreases with increasing temperature, a trend consistent with the expected temperature-dependent variation in viscosity [21].

The X-ray diffraction patterns presented in Fig. 3 depict the results obtained for pure zinc oxide and varying concentrations (0.018 M, 0.05 M, 0.07 M, and 0.13 M) of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles. All samples exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at specific angles, including 31.7°, 34.4°, 36.2°, 47.4°, 56.5°, 62.8°, 66.4°, 67.9°, 68.9°, and 76.8°, corresponding to various crystallographic planes ((100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), and (202)). These diffraction patterns align with the anticipated polycrystalline hexagonal wurtzite structure, confirming the purity of the nanoparticles. Notably, the introduction of aluminum dopants is evidenced by a unique peak at 33.1°, with intensity increasing in correspondence with higher doping concentrations, as denoted by a triangle in Fig. 3. The incorporation of Al3+ ions into Zn2+ interstitial sites results in the formation of di-aluminum zinc tetraoxide (Al2O4Zn), a structure further corroborated by XRD findings. The impact of doping concentrations on particle size was assessed using the Debye-Scherrer equation. The presence of aluminum within zinc oxide leads to reduced peak intensities and increased full width at half-maximum (FWHM), indicating either smaller particle sizes or reduced crystallinity. This phenomenon is attributed to the smaller ionic radii of Al3+ ions (0.535 Å) in comparison to Zn2+ ions (0.74 Å) [38]. When Al3+ ions replace Zn2+ ions within the unit cell, it shortens the C-axis length, ultimately decreasing the crystallite size [39]. Also, when the aluminium doping concentration goes from 0.018 M to 0.13 M, the sizes of the crystallites get bigger. This makes it easier for the crystallites to grow.

From the nano-to the macroscale, topography and surface morphology are significant characteristics of metals and oxide materials that result from their synthesis and chemical composition. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of pure zinc oxide and all different concentrations of Al-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles are presented in Fig. 4 (a–e). As the amount of doping increased, there was a greater tendency for nanoparticles to stick together. However, adding PVP effectively caused steric repulsion between nanoparticles, which stopped them from sticking together and ensured agglomeration-free Brownian motion.The morphology of the pristine ZnO nanoparticles has shown hexagonal sticks. Further adding the Al3+ ion into ZnO lattice the morphology turn into spherical to agglomerated particles. This exhibits the Al3+ ions effectively incorporated into the host atoms. Fig. 5 shows the EDAX spectra of 0.13 M Al doped ZnO nanoparticle. It clearly exhibits the presents of Zn, O, and Al, respectively. It is also good agreement with the XRD results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ANN modeling of thermal conductivity and viscosity

ce:section.ce:para: ANNs are neural networks that use numerical coding to mimic how the human brain functions. Neurons, the basic processing units, compose it. The three primary components of a neural network are the transfer function, weights, and bias. An input layer, an output layer, and several hidden layers in between make up a neural network. The input nodes receive data from the user and then use hidden layers to send it to the output layer. The two primary processing stages of ANN are training and testing. The architecture of ANN, as well as other network factors, ensures its proper operation. Herein, the authors use the ANN technology for Al-doped ZnO nanofluids. The entire simulation process is presented as Flow chart in Fig. 6 .

The complicated changes in thermal conductivity and viscosity in zinc oxide nanofluids due to aluminium doping concentration, nanoparticle weight percentage, and temperature make it impossible to use standard nonlinear curve fitting for mathematical formulation. This is because the R-squared value (the correlation between experimental and predicted outcomes) is less than 0.6. To address this, the potent technique of Artificial Neural Network (ANN) was effectively employed to model the intricate relationships between thermal conductivity, viscosity, doping concentration, nanoparticle weight percentage, and temperature. Fig. 7 depicts the ANN architecture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the best multilayer perceptron (MLP) model topology
ce:section.ce:para: Several statistical accuracy indices, including R-squared and Mean Squared Error (MSE), were utilized to gauge the precision of ANN predictions. MSE calculates the mean squared difference between experimental and predicted outcomes, while R-squared values measure the correlation between these outcomes. High R-squared values and low MSE values are indicative of robust prediction models [36]. Determining the optimal number of hidden neurons is a crucial aspect of ANN prediction. Statistically, increasing the number of hidden neurons enhances the MLP's predictive accuracy. Fig. 8 (a & b) illustrates the variation in overall R-squared and MSE with respect to the number of hidden neurons for both thermal conductivities and viscosities. In both cases, the performance of the ANN model in predicting thermal conductivities and viscosities significantly improves up to six hidden neurons. Beyond this point, increasing the number of hidden neurons does not lead to further improvements in accuracy, instead amplifying network complexity. Consequently, six hidden neurons are deemed the optimal topology for predicting thermal conductivities and viscosities in pure zinc oxide and Al-doped zinc oxide nanofluids, as evidenced in Fig. 8(a and b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-squared and MSE results for 40 experimental data sets of thermal conductivity and viscosity using MLP with optimized hidden neurons (6 hidden neurons)
ce:section.ce:para: In machine learning, 75–80 % of data sets are used in training and others in testing [35–40]. In this model, out of 40 experimental data sets of thermal conductivity and viscosity, 70 % of data sets (30 experimental data) are used in training, 10 % of data sets (4 experimental data) are used in validation, and 15 % of data sets (6 experimental data) are used in testing. The optimized value of R-Squared correlation for thermal conductivities and viscosities are 0.9992 and 0.9927, obtained in the optimized hidden neurons of 6 in the count. In all cases, the target in the x-axis represents experimental outcomes of thermal conductivity and viscosity, whereas output in the y-axis represents the ANN prediction of thermal conductivity and viscosity as shown in Fig. 9 (a &b).
In Fig. 10 , the absolute values of errors of ANN and fitting method have been compared. It can be seen that the ANN method has smaller absolute values of errors compared to the fitting method.

We deployed an advanced multilayer perceptron-type artificial neural network (ANN) model to facilitate precise predictions and computations of nanofluid thermal and viscous behaviour. ZnO and Al-doped ZnO particles were chemically synthesized. XRD spectrographs confirmed the phase purity and structure of the prepared ZnO and Al-doped ZnO nanoparticles. SEM and EDX analyses revealed the surface morphology and the presence of elements in the samples.

Six hidden neurons in this model's optimal structure ensured accurate modeling of the thermal and viscous properties of nanofluids. By comparing the experimental findings with the anticipated results, we can conclude that a well-trained artificial neural network (ANN) model is an effective tool for forecasting tribological behavior. The incorporation of aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles resulted in a significant improvement in thermal conductivity. The addition of 1 % of 0.13 M aluminium-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles to the base fluid resulted in an amazing 64 % increase in thermal conductivity compared to the base fluid. Additionally, adding 1 % of 0.13 M aluminum-doped zinc oxide nanoparticles to the base fluid made it 44 % better at conducting heat compared to adding 1 % pure zinc oxide to the base fluid. In future; we can apply this ANN network to estimate the velocity of flowing nanofluids, measure density, and measure other parameters.

A. Jeyaraj: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. P. Sakthivel: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. K. Saravanakumar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aravinth Raj Arivalagan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. V. Karthik: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Arun Thirumurugan: Data curation, Investigation, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
Data will be made available on reasonable request.
No Funding is received for the submitted work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: There is no acknowledgement for the present work.
